questions.question: You need a value that is callable **and** carries an ordinary property (a hybrid type). Which TypeScript construct can express this, when the plain arrow function type expression cannot?
options: An object type containing a call signature, e.g. `{ (a: string): number; meta: string }`
A `type` alias wrapping the arrow form, e.g. `type T = (a: string) => number`
A union of the arrow type and an object type, e.g. `((a: string) => number) | { meta: string }`
A `readonly` arrow type, e.g. `readonly (a: string) => number`
questions.explanation: An object type with a parenthesized call signature can sit alongside ordinary properties, which the terse arrow form `(a: T) => R` cannot. A union does not make a single value both callable and property-bearing, and `readonly` on a function type is not valid syntax for this purpose.

questions.question: In a function type expression like `(value: string) => number`, the parameter name `value` is significant for compatibility, so the implementing function must use the same name.
questions.explanation: Parameter names in a function type are documentation only. Compatibility is determined by position and type; the implementation may name the parameter anything.

questions.question: Given `function greet(name: string, title?: string)`, what is the type of `title` **inside the function body**?
options: `string | undefined`
`string`
`string | null`
`undefined`
questions.explanation: An optional parameter `x?: T` has the in-body type `T | undefined` and may be omitted at the call site. A default parameter (`= d`) is what strips the `undefined`, leaving just `T` — but that is not the case here.

questions.question: Which parameter list is **illegal** in TypeScript?
options: `(title?: string, name: string)`
`(name: string, title?: string)`
`(first: number, ...rest: number[])`
`(host: string, port: number = 443)`
questions.explanation: A required parameter cannot follow an optional one, so `(title?: string, name: string)` is rejected. Optional and default parameters may follow required ones, and a rest parameter sitting last is fine.

questions.question: A function that returns a `number` is assignable to a variable typed `() => void`; the returned value is simply ignored at the type level.
questions.explanation: This is the contextual-void rule: any function type whose return is `R` is assignable to `() => void`, with the return discarded. It is exactly why `arr.forEach(x => arr2.push(x))` type-checks even though `push` returns a `number`.

questions.subtopic: the void quirk — assignability vs direct annotation
questions.question: Consider the snippet below. Which statement correctly describes its compilation behavior?
options: Line A compiles (the `42` is ignored); line B errors because a value is returned from a directly-annotated `void` body
Both lines compile; the `42` is ignored in each case
Both lines error because `number` is not assignable to `void`
Line A errors; line B compiles because `void` permits any return value
questions.explanation: The leniency is about assignability of function *types*: assigning a value-returning function into a `() => void` slot (line A) is allowed. A function whose body is *directly* annotated `(): void` (line B) still rejects an explicit `return 42` — `Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'void'`.

questions.question: A function whose body is `throw new Error(msg);` with **no return-type annotation** will have which inferred return type?
options: `void`
`never`
`undefined`
`unknown`
questions.explanation: Without an annotation, an always-throwing function infers `void`. You only get `never` when you *annotate* `: never`; that explicit `never` is what powers exhaustiveness checks in `default` branches.

questions.question: What is `ReturnType<typeof flag>` for the function below?
options: `"on" | "off"`
`string`
`boolean`
questions.explanation: Return positions keep literal unions rather than widening to `string`: each conditional branch produces a literal type and the result is their union `"on" | "off"`. Widening to `string` would happen in a mutable `let` position, not in the inferred return type.

questions.question: Which statements about the `this` parameter in TypeScript are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It is declared as a fake first parameter named `this` and is erased at emit, never counting as a real argument
An arrow function cannot declare a `this` parameter
`ThisParameterType<T>` extracts the `this` type and `OmitThisParameter<T>` strips it
Callers must explicitly pass a value for the `this` parameter as the first argument
Under `noImplicitThis`, an untyped `this` in a standalone function is silently inferred as `unknown`
questions.explanation: The `this` parameter is a fake, erased first parameter that callers never pass; arrows capture lexical `this` and so cannot declare one; the two utility types read and strip it. Callers do not pass `this` as an argument, and under `noImplicitThis` an untyped `this` is an *error*, not inferred as `unknown`.

questions.question: How does the compiler resolve which overload signature applies to a call?
options: Top to bottom; the first overload whose parameters the arguments satisfy wins
It picks the most specific matching overload regardless of declaration order
Bottom to top; the last matching overload wins
It matches against the implementation signature, since that is the most general
questions.explanation: Overload resolution is top-down and stops at the first matching signature, which is why you order from most specific to most general. The implementation signature is never a candidate for callers.

questions.question: Given the overloaded `parse` below, what happens when a consumer calls `parse(true)`?
options: It is a compile error: no overload matches a `boolean` argument
It compiles, matching the implementation signature `string | number`
It compiles and returns `string[] | number`
It compiles because `boolean` is assignable to `number`
questions.explanation: Callers only see the two public overload signatures (`string` and `number`); neither accepts a `boolean`, so the call errors. The implementation signature (`string | number`) is hidden from consumers and is never a resolution candidate.

questions.question: You want a factory parameter that accepts "a class itself" so the body can call `new` on it. Which parameter type expresses this?
options: `Ctor: new (name: string) => T`
`Ctor: (name: string) => T`
`Ctor: T`
`Ctor: InstanceType<T>`
questions.explanation: A construct signature `new (args) => InstanceType` types the static (constructor) side of a class, so passing `MyClass` itself type-checks. A plain call signature `(name) => T` types an ordinary function, not something invoked with `new`, and `InstanceType<T>` is an *instance*, not the constructor.

questions.subtopic: construct signature accepts class not instance
questions.question: For a parameter typed `Ctor: new (name: string) => T`, which arguments type-check? (Select all that apply.)
options: The class value `Dog` (where `class Dog { constructor(public name: string) {} }`)
Any constructor whose static side matches `new (name: string) => T`
An instance produced by `new Dog("Rex")`
The string `"Dog"` naming the class
questions.explanation: A class value's static side is a construct signature, so the class itself (`Dog`) and any matching constructor fit the parameter. An *instance* (`new Dog(...)`) is not a constructor, and a string naming the class is unrelated to the type.

questions.question: What is `ReturnType<typeof fetchUser>` for the async function below?
options: `Promise<{ id: number; name: string }>`
`Promise<Promise<{ id: number; name: string }>>`
`{ id: number; name: string }`
`Promise<unknown>`
questions.explanation: An async function always returns a `Promise`, and a `Promise` returned from the body is flattened (the async wrapping applies `Awaited` to the body's return), so it does not nest. The result is a single-level `Promise<{ id: number; name: string }>`.

questions.question: What is the type of `value`?
options: `string`
`number`
`string | number`
questions.explanation: `T` is inferred as `{ a: number; b: string }` and `K` as the literal `"b"`; the indexed-access return type `T[K]` resolves to `{ a: number; b: string }["b"]`, which is `string`. The constraint both restricts `key` to existing keys and lets the result be typed precisely.

questions.question: With `strict` enabled, why does line A compile while line B errors?
options: Method-shorthand parameters are checked bivariantly (a legacy carve-out), but `strictFunctionTypes` checks function-type-property parameters contravariantly, rejecting the narrower `Dog` parameter
Line B errors because `Dog` is not a subtype of `Animal`
Method shorthand is checked contravariantly while function-type properties are bivariant, so only line B is rejected
Both forms are bivariant, but `strictFunctionTypes` only applies to top-level function declarations, so line B's nested property is rejected
questions.explanation: `strictFunctionTypes` (part of `strict`) tightens *function-type-property* parameters to be contravariant, rejecting a handler that needs a narrower `Dog` where one taking any `Animal` is expected (line B). *Method-shorthand* parameters intentionally stay bivariant (line A). The mnemonic is: arrow property = strict, method shorthand = loose.

questions.question: When is reaching for function **overloads** the right tool, versus a single union or generic signature? (Select all that apply.)
options: Use overloads when the return type genuinely depends on the argument shape in a way one signature cannot express
Prefer a union parameter or generic when the parameter types vary but the return type does not depend on the argument shape
Overloads are appropriate when there is a finite, fixed set of argument/return combinations
Always prefer overloads over a generic because they produce clearer compiler errors
Use overloads whenever a function takes more than one parameter type, since unions are never allowed in parameters
questions.explanation: Overloads earn their keep when the return type varies with the argument shape and the combinations are finite and fixed; otherwise a union parameter or generic is simpler and gives better errors. Overloads are not universally clearer, and union types are perfectly valid as parameters.

questions.question: In `function connect(host: string, port: number = 443)`, the in-body type of `port` is `number | undefined`, and you should also mark it optional with `?` for callers.
questions.explanation: A default parameter is already implicitly optional for callers, and its in-body type is `number` because the default strips `undefined`. Writing both `?` and a default (`port?: number = 443`) is a compile error.
